---
title: "Practice 3"
subtitle: "Building our own `proportions_ztest` for an A/B experiment"
author: "Ihor Miroshnychenko"
institute: Kyiv School of Economics
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    code-fold: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import norm, binom

np.random.seed(73)

turquoise = "#20B2AA"
red       = "#fb6107"
blue      = "#181485"
gray      = "#A0A0A0"
```

# What we are doing today {.unnumbered}

In the lecture we built the Z-test piece by piece: the normal
distribution, the CLT, the Z-statistic, continuity correction, and
finally the **two-sample Z-test for proportions** with a confidence
interval. Today we **assemble all of it into one tool** --- first our
own `my_z_test()`, then its two-sample sibling
`my_two_sample_z_test()`, similar in spirit to
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportions_ztest`.

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Rules of the game
- We do **not** use the ready-made `proportions_ztest` --- we write
  everything ourselves via `scipy.stats.norm` (`norm.cdf`, `norm.ppf`).
- Each version is a small extension of the previous one.
- Every final step (Final, full cycle) **reuses** previous functions
  instead of duplicating them.
- After every version there is a **"Your turn"** block with a small
  exercise.
:::

---

# The case: GreenBowl food delivery app

We work on **GreenBowl** --- a healthy-food delivery app. The product
team added a **"1-click reorder"** button on the home screen: a single
tap re-creates the previous order. Before rolling the feature out to
everyone, we want to confirm that it **actually lifts the repeat-order
metric**.

::: {.border}
- **Unit of observation:** one user after the first order.
- **Success metric:** the user placed another order within 7 days (1) or
  not (0).
- **Model:** $X_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\mu)$, success count
  $T = \sum X_i \sim \text{Binomial}(n, \mu)$.
- **Baseline:** $\mu_0 = 0.10$ (3 months of historical data).
- **Business MDE:** an effect smaller than +1 pp is not worth shipping.
- **Direction of interest:** only upward → **one-sided test**.
- **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Why a Z-test, not an exact binomial test?
GreenBowl has hundreds of thousands of users. At those sample sizes the
normal approximation is very accurate, and the Z-test is orders of
magnitude faster than the exact binomial on $n > 10^4$. That speed is
the reason the Z-test exists at all.
:::

---

# Version 1 --- Z-statistic and p-value

The simplest version: given $n$, $\mu_0$, and the observed count $q$,
return the one-sided $p$-value for $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$.

$$
Z = \sqrt{n}\,\dfrac{\hat{p} - \mu_0}{\sqrt{\mu_0(1-\mu_0)}},\qquad
p\text{-value} = 1 - \Phi(Z)
$$

```{python}
#| label: v1

def my_z_test(q, n, mu0=0.5):
    """
    Version 1. One-sided Z-test for a proportion, H1: mu > mu0.
    """
    p_hat = q / n
    se    = np.sqrt(mu0 * (1 - mu0) / n)
    z     = (p_hat - mu0) / se
    return 1 - norm.cdf(z)
```

Let's try it on our case. Marketing brought **2 000 new users** into the
test cohort, and **226 of them** placed a repeat order within 7 days.

```{python}
#| label: v1-demo

p_val = my_z_test(q=226, n=2000, mu0=0.10)
print(f"p-value = {p_val:.4f}")
```

$p < 0.05$ --- we have evidence in favour of $H_1$. The observed 11.3%
conversion is statistically higher than the historical 10%. But **do
not ship yet** --- the effect may sit on the edge of business
significance.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Your turn 1
Call `my_z_test` for $q \in \{200, 210, 220, 230, 240\}$ with $n=2000$,
$\mu_0=0.10$. How does the $p$-value change? Find the smallest $q$ for
which $p \leq 0.05$.
:::

---

# Version 2 --- three types of alternative

The anti-fraud team sometimes wants to check whether the metric
**dropped** (one-sided `less`); the product team occasionally wants
"did anything change at all" (two-sided). Add an `alternative` parameter.

```{python}
#| label: v2

def my_z_test(q, n, mu0=0.5, alternative="greater"):
    """
    Version 2. Adds an `alternative` parameter.

    alternative : {"greater", "less", "two-sided"}
    """
    p_hat = q / n
    se    = np.sqrt(mu0 * (1 - mu0) / n)
    z     = (p_hat - mu0) / se

    if alternative == "greater":
        return 1 - norm.cdf(z)
    if alternative == "less":
        return norm.cdf(z)
    if alternative == "two-sided":
        return 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z)))

    raise ValueError(f"Unknown alternative: {alternative!r}")
```

```{python}
#| label: v2-demo

for alt in ("greater", "less", "two-sided"):
    p = my_z_test(q=226, n=2000, mu0=0.10, alternative=alt)
    print(f"alternative={alt:>10s}  →  p-value = {p:.4f}")
```

For the same data the **one-sided $p$-value is exactly half** of the
two-sided one --- because the normal distribution is symmetric. This is
not a coincidence, it follows from $2 \cdot (1 - \Phi(|z|))$.

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## When two-sided is mandatory
If a product has a real risk of causing harm (e.g. a new paywall that
could either lift or sink conversion) --- use two-sided. In the
GreenBowl case the risk is one-sided: a worse button we simply do not
ship.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Your turn 2
For $q=226$, $n=2000$, $\mu_0=0.10$ verify that
`p_two_sided == 2 * p_greater` **exactly**. Why is it a strict equality
in the Z-test but only approximate in the binomial test?
:::

---

# Version 3 --- continuity correction

For small $n$ the Z-test systematically underestimates the $p$-value
relative to the exact binomial. In the lecture we patched this by
shifting the normal by $-0.5$. We add a `use_cc` flag.

```{python}
#| label: v3

def my_z_test(q, n, mu0=0.5, alternative="greater", use_cc=False):
    """
    Version 3. Adds continuity correction (recommended for n < ~100).
    """
    p_hat = q / n
    if use_cc:
        if alternative == "greater":
            p_hat = (q - 0.5) / n
        elif alternative == "less":
            p_hat = (q + 0.5) / n
        else:
            p_hat = (q - 0.5) / n if p_hat >= mu0 else (q + 0.5) / n

    se = np.sqrt(mu0 * (1 - mu0) / n)
    z  = (p_hat - mu0) / se

    if alternative == "greater":
        return 1 - norm.cdf(z)
    if alternative == "less":
        return norm.cdf(z)
    if alternative == "two-sided":
        return 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z)))

    raise ValueError(f"Unknown alternative: {alternative!r}")
```

**Scenario A.** MVP launch on $n=30$ beta-testers, $q=19$ (the lecture
example):

```{python}
#| label: v3-demo-small

p_naive_small = my_z_test(q=19, n=30, mu0=0.5, use_cc=False)
p_cc_small    = my_z_test(q=19, n=30, mu0=0.5, use_cc=True)
p_exact_small = 1 - binom.cdf(19 - 1, 30, 0.5)

print("MVP launch, n=30:")
print(f"  Z-test without cc:  p = {p_naive_small:.4f}")
print(f"  Z-test with cc:     p = {p_cc_small:.4f}")
print(f"  Exact binomial:     p = {p_exact_small:.4f}")
```

The correction pulls the Z-test much closer to the exact result ---
$p$-value goes from 0.07 to 0.10.

**Scenario B.** Full GreenBowl launch, $n=2000$, $q=226$:

```{python}
#| label: v3-demo-big

p_naive_big = my_z_test(q=226, n=2000, mu0=0.10, use_cc=False)
p_cc_big    = my_z_test(q=226, n=2000, mu0=0.10, use_cc=True)

print("GreenBowl scale, n=2000:")
print(f"  Z-test without cc:  p = {p_naive_big:.6f}")
print(f"  Z-test with cc:     p = {p_cc_big:.6f}")
print(f"  Difference:         {abs(p_naive_big - p_cc_big):.6f}")
```

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Takeaway
Continuity correction is a "crutch" for small samples. At GreenBowl
scale the difference shows up in the 5-6th decimal and does not affect
any business decision. This is exactly why the Z-test (without
correction) is the standard tool for large $n$, while small MVP
launches either use the correction or switch to the exact binomial.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Your turn 3
Find the smallest $n$ for which the difference between Z-test
with/without correction drops below $0.001$. At what $n$ does the
correction stop being necessary?
:::

---

# Version 4 --- confidence interval for $\mu$

Instead of yes/no the business wants a **range** for the true
conversion. The simplest CI is the Wald one:

$$
\hat{p} \pm z_{1-\alpha/2}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}
$$

```{python}
#| label: v4

def my_z_test(q, n, mu0=0.5, alternative="greater",
              use_cc=False, alpha=0.05, conf_int=False):
    """
    Version 4. Adds conf_int=True → returns a dict with p-value and CI.
    """
    p_hat_raw = q / n

    p_hat = p_hat_raw
    if use_cc:
        if alternative == "greater":
            p_hat = (q - 0.5) / n
        elif alternative == "less":
            p_hat = (q + 0.5) / n
        else:
            p_hat = (q - 0.5) / n if p_hat_raw >= mu0 else (q + 0.5) / n

    se_null = np.sqrt(mu0 * (1 - mu0) / n)
    z       = (p_hat - mu0) / se_null

    if alternative == "greater":
        p_value = 1 - norm.cdf(z)
    elif alternative == "less":
        p_value = norm.cdf(z)
    elif alternative == "two-sided":
        p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z)))
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown alternative: {alternative!r}")

    if not conf_int:
        return p_value

    # Wald CI uses SE based on p_hat (not mu0!)
    se_emp = np.sqrt(p_hat_raw * (1 - p_hat_raw) / n)
    z_crit = norm.ppf(1 - alpha / 2)
    ci_low  = p_hat_raw - z_crit * se_emp
    ci_high = p_hat_raw + z_crit * se_emp

    return {"p_value": p_value, "ci_low": ci_low, "ci_high": ci_high}
```

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Subtle but important: SE for the test vs SE for the CI
For the **test** use $\sqrt{\mu_0(1-\mu_0)/n}$ (we assume $H_0$).
For the **CI** use $\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}$ (we describe what we
observed, no assumption). The same pooled-vs-unpooled idea will reappear
in the two-sample test.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## When Wald CI breaks
The Wald interval can **fall outside $[0, 1]$** for extreme $\hat p$
(e.g. $\hat p = 0.02$ on $n=50$). For $p \lesssim 5\%$ or
$p \gtrsim 95\%$ switch to Wilson or Clopper-Pearson:
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint(q, n, method="wilson")`.
:::

```{python}
#| label: v4-demo

result = my_z_test(q=226, n=2000, mu0=0.10,
                   alternative="two-sided",
                   alpha=0.05, conf_int=True)

print(f"p-value:        {result['p_value']:.4f}")
print(f"95% CI for mu:  [{result['ci_low']:.4f}, {result['ci_high']:.4f}]")
```

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Interpretation for the PM
- **Point estimate:** $\hat{\mu} = 226/2000 = 11.3\%$.
- **CI:** with probability $\geq 95\%$ repeated experiments would
  produce intervals that cover the true $\mu$.
- **$\mu_0 = 10\%$ is outside the CI** → reject $H_0$. The baseline does
  not explain what we observed.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Your turn 4
For $q=226$, $n=2000$ build the one-sided lower bound (`alternative="greater"`).
Does it cross the business threshold $\mu_0 + \text{MDE} = 11\%$? What
does that mean for the ship decision?
:::

---

# From one sample to two {.unnumbered}

`my_z_test` is the right tool when you have a **fixed baseline** ---
e.g. SLA monitoring ("is the production conversion $\geq 10\%$ this
week?") or comparing this week against history. But a **proper A/B test**
runs two cohorts in parallel --- and both contain noise.

We add a sibling function `my_two_sample_z_test`. **The old function
does not go away** --- it stays useful for baseline-style scenarios:
SLA monitoring, regression detection, sanity checks against history.

---

# Version 5 --- two-sample Z-test

Instead of a fixed $\mu_0$ we use a **pooled** estimate:

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{pool}} = \dfrac{x_1 + x_2}{n_1 + n_2},\qquad
SE_{\text{pool}} = \sqrt{\hat{p}_{\text{pool}}(1-\hat{p}_{\text{pool}})
\left(\tfrac{1}{n_1} + \tfrac{1}{n_2}\right)}.
$$

```{python}
#| label: v5

def my_two_sample_z_test(x1, n1, x2, n2,
                         alternative="two-sided",
                         alpha=0.05, conf_int=False):
    """
    Two-sample Z-test for the difference in proportions.
    Convention: alternative is about p1 - p2 (control - test if you pass them in that order).
    """
    p1, p2 = x1 / n1, x2 / n2

    # Pooled SE — for the test
    p_pool  = (x1 + x2) / (n1 + n2)
    se_pool = np.sqrt(p_pool * (1 - p_pool) * (1 / n1 + 1 / n2))
    z       = (p1 - p2) / se_pool

    if alternative == "greater":
        p_value = 1 - norm.cdf(z)
    elif alternative == "less":
        p_value = norm.cdf(z)
    elif alternative == "two-sided":
        p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z)))
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown alternative: {alternative!r}")

    if not conf_int:
        return p_value

    # Unpooled SE — for the CI
    se_unp = np.sqrt(p1 * (1 - p1) / n1 + p2 * (1 - p2) / n2)
    z_crit = norm.ppf(1 - alpha / 2)
    diff   = p1 - p2

    return {
        "z_stat": z,
        "p_value": p_value,
        "estimate_diff": diff,
        "ci_low":  diff - z_crit * se_unp,
        "ci_high": diff + z_crit * se_unp,
    }
```

## Demo: a proper A/B for GreenBowl

We deliberately pick **numbers on the edge of the business MDE** so we
see a real "ship/no-ship" dilemma rather than an obvious case.

```{python}
#| label: v5-demo

# Control: old version
# Test:    new "1-click reorder" button
x1, n1 = 200, 2000   # control: 10.0%
x2, n2 = 240, 2000   # test:    12.0%

res = my_two_sample_z_test(x1, n1, x2, n2,
                           alternative="less",   # H1: control < test
                           alpha=0.05, conf_int=True)

p1, p2 = x1/n1, x2/n2
print(f"p1 (control) = {p1:.4f}")
print(f"p2 (test)    = {p2:.4f}")
print(f"lift         = {p2-p1:+.4f} ({(p2-p1)*100:+.2f} pp)")
print(f"Z = {res['z_stat']:.4f},  p-value = {res['p_value']:.4f}")
print(f"95% CI for p1 - p2 = [{res['ci_low']:+.4f}, {res['ci_high']:+.4f}]")
print(f"95% CI for lift    = [{-res['ci_high']*100:+.2f}, {-res['ci_low']*100:+.2f}] pp")
```

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Read the result carefully
- **Test:** $p < 0.05$ → reject $H_0$. The lift is statistically
  significant.
- **CI for lift:** approximately $[+0.06,\ +3.94]$ pp. The **lower
  bound** is only +0.06 pp, well **below the business MDE of +1 pp**.
- **PM dilemma:** statistically there is a lift, but practical
  significance is uncertain --- the true lift could be 0.1 pp, not worth
  the deploy. Options: collect more data, or accept the risk of a
  "potentially ephemeral" win.
:::

## Cross-check against the industry standard

```{python}
#| label: v5-statsmodels

from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportions_ztest

# statsmodels convention: alternative="smaller" → H1: p1 < p2 (our "less")
z_sm, p_sm = proportions_ztest([x1, x2], [n1, n2], alternative="smaller")
print(f"statsmodels:  Z = {z_sm:.4f},  p = {p_sm:.4f}")
print(f"ours:         Z = {res['z_stat']:.4f},  p = {res['p_value']:.4f}")
```

The numbers match to 4 decimals --- the implementation is correct.

## Unbalanced groups (90/10 split)

In production new features are often rolled out to a smaller share of
traffic for safety. The function has to work for unequal $n_1, n_2$.

```{python}
#| label: v5-unbalanced

x1_u, n1_u = 1820, 18_000   # control 90% of traffic: 10.11%
x2_u, n2_u =  243,  2_000   # test 10% of traffic:    12.15%

res_u = my_two_sample_z_test(x1_u, n1_u, x2_u, n2_u,
                             alternative="less", conf_int=True)
print(f"Unbalanced 90/10: lift = {(x2_u/n2_u - x1_u/n1_u)*100:+.2f} pp")
print(f"p-value = {res_u['p_value']:.4f}")
print(f"CI width = {(res_u['ci_high'] - res_u['ci_low'])*100:.2f} pp")
```

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Why unbalanced is more expensive
Total sample $20\,000$ instead of $4\,000$, but the CI is **wider** than
for a balanced $2\,000 \times 2$. Reason: CI width is dominated by the
smaller group. For the same precision unbalanced designs need more
users overall.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Your turn 5
Fix the total sample at $20\,000$ and try control/test ratios of
50/50, 70/30, 90/10. How does the CI width change? Confirm that 50/50
is optimal.
:::

---

# Version 6 --- power and sample-size planning

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Power formula for the two-sample Z (one-sided)
$$
\text{Power}(\text{greater}) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(
  \dfrac{z_{1-\alpha} \cdot SE_{\text{null}} - (p_1 - p_2)}{SE_{\text{alt}}}
\right),
$$
with $SE_{\text{null}}$ computed at $\bar p = (p_1+p_2)/2$ (pooled under
$H_0$) and $SE_{\text{alt}}$ at the actual $p_1, p_2$.
:::

```{python}
#| label: v6-power

def my_two_sample_z_power(p1, p2, n1, n2,
                          alpha=0.05, alternative="two-sided"):
    """
    Power of the two-sample Z-test on proportions.
    Convention: Z = (p1 - p2) / SE_pool; alternative is about p1 - p2.
    """
    delta   = p1 - p2
    p_bar   = (p1 + p2) / 2
    se_null = np.sqrt(p_bar * (1 - p_bar) * (1 / n1 + 1 / n2))
    se_alt  = np.sqrt(p1 * (1 - p1) / n1 + p2 * (1 - p2) / n2)

    if alternative == "greater":
        z_a = norm.ppf(1 - alpha)
        return 1 - norm.cdf((z_a * se_null - delta) / se_alt)
    if alternative == "less":
        z_a = norm.ppf(1 - alpha)
        return norm.cdf((-z_a * se_null - delta) / se_alt)
    if alternative == "two-sided":
        z_a   = norm.ppf(1 - alpha / 2)
        right = 1 - norm.cdf((z_a * se_null - delta) / se_alt)
        left  = norm.cdf((-z_a * se_null - delta) / se_alt)
        return right + left

    raise ValueError(f"Unknown alternative: {alternative!r}")
```

`plan_ab_experiment` via **bisection** --- faster and more precise than
a grid:

```{python}
#| label: v6-plan

def plan_ab_experiment(p1, mde, alpha=0.05, target_power=0.8,
                       alternative="less", n_lo=10, n_hi=500_000):
    """
    How many users per group are needed to detect an effect of size `mde`?
    Bisection: ~log2(n_hi) steps instead of thousands.
    """
    # alternative gives the effect sign: "less" → p2 > p1; "greater" → p2 < p1
    sign = +1 if alternative != "greater" else -1
    p2   = p1 + sign * mde

    # Sanity: is n_hi enough?
    if my_two_sample_z_power(p1, p2, n_hi, n_hi, alpha, alternative) < target_power:
        return None

    while n_hi - n_lo > 1:
        n_mid = (n_lo + n_hi) // 2
        if my_two_sample_z_power(p1, p2, n_mid, n_mid, alpha, alternative) >= target_power:
            n_hi = n_mid
        else:
            n_lo = n_mid
    return n_hi
```

Apply to our case. Baseline 10%, business MDE +1 pp.

```{python}
#| label: v6-demo

# Scenario A: marketing guarantees 2000 users per group. What can we detect?
for n in (1000, 2000, 5000, 10_000):
    pw = my_two_sample_z_power(p1=0.10, p2=0.11, n1=n, n2=n,
                               alpha=0.05, alternative="less")
    print(f"n = {n:>6d}  →  power(+1 pp) = {pw:.3f}")

# Scenario B: PM wants to reliably detect +1 pp at power = 0.8
n_needed = plan_ab_experiment(p1=0.10, mde=0.01,
                              alpha=0.05, target_power=0.8,
                              alternative="less")
print(f"\nUsers per group required to detect +1 pp: {n_needed}")
```

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Key trade-off
- Halving the MDE ⇒ the required sample size grows **roughly fourfold**.
- On a baseline of 10%, detecting +0.5 pp at power 0.8 takes tens of
  thousands of users per group. In product terms: 2-4 weeks of waiting,
  or dropping the "small" effect as a business goal.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Your turn 6
Plot a curve: X --- users per group (from 500 to 30 000), Y --- power
at MDE = +1 pp. Find the minimum $n$ for which power $\geq 0.8$ and
verify against `plan_ab_experiment`.
:::

---

# Final --- the complete `my_ab_test`

We collect everything together **by reusing** V5 and V6. No duplicated
code --- only calls to the ready functions.

```{python}
#| label: vf

def my_ab_test(x1, n1, x2, n2,
               alternative="two-sided",
               alpha=0.05, target_power=0.8):
    """
    Full report for an A/B test. Reuses my_two_sample_z_test + my_two_sample_z_power.
    """
    # 1) Test + CI (reuse V5)
    test = my_two_sample_z_test(x1, n1, x2, n2,
                                alternative=alternative,
                                alpha=alpha, conf_int=True)

    p1, p2 = x1 / n1, x2 / n2
    lift   = p2 - p1

    # 2) Power at the observed data (reuse V6)
    power_obs = my_two_sample_z_power(p1, p2, n1, n2, alpha, alternative)

    # 3) MDE detectable at the current n_avg (bisection)
    n_avg = (n1 + n2) // 2
    sign  = +1 if alternative != "greater" else -1

    def has_power(mde):
        return my_two_sample_z_power(p1, p1 + sign * mde, n_avg, n_avg,
                                     alpha, alternative) >= target_power

    mde_at_n = None
    lo, hi = 0.0, min(p1, 1 - p1)
    if has_power(hi):
        for _ in range(40):
            mid = (lo + hi) / 2
            if has_power(mid):
                hi = mid
            else:
                lo = mid
        mde_at_n = hi

    decision = "REJECT H0" if test["p_value"] <= alpha else "do not reject H0"
    summary_lines = [
        f"n1={n1}, x1={x1} (p1={p1:.4f})  vs  n2={n2}, x2={x2} (p2={p2:.4f})",
        f"lift = {lift:+.4f} ({lift*100:+.2f} pp)",
        f"Z = {test['z_stat']:+.3f},  p-value = {test['p_value']:.4f}  →  {decision}",
        f"{int((1-alpha)*100)}% CI for p1-p2: [{test['ci_low']:+.4f}, {test['ci_high']:+.4f}]",
        f"power at observed = {power_obs:.3f}",
    ]
    if mde_at_n is not None:
        summary_lines.append(
            f"MDE at n_avg={n_avg}, power={target_power}: {mde_at_n*100:.2f} pp"
        )

    return {
        "p1": p1, "p2": p2, "lift": lift,
        "z_stat": test["z_stat"],
        "p_value": test["p_value"],
        "reject": test["p_value"] <= alpha,
        "ci_diff": (test["ci_low"], test["ci_high"]),
        "power_at_observed": power_obs,
        "mde_at_n": mde_at_n,
        "summary": "\n".join(summary_lines),
    }
```

---

# Sanity check: A/A simulation

Before trusting any A/B conclusions, **stress-test the infrastructure
with an A/A run** --- the same test applied to two groups with **the
same** $p$. If everything is wired correctly, the false-positive rate
must be close to $\alpha$.

```{python}
#| label: aa-sim

def simulate_AA(p_shared, n_per_group, n_simulations=2000,
                alpha=0.05, alternative="two-sided"):
    """
    A/A simulation: estimates the empirical FPR.
    """
    rejections = 0
    for _ in range(n_simulations):
        s1 = binom.rvs(1, p_shared, size=n_per_group)
        s2 = binom.rvs(1, p_shared, size=n_per_group)
        p_val = my_two_sample_z_test(int(s1.sum()), n_per_group,
                                     int(s2.sum()), n_per_group,
                                     alternative=alternative)
        if p_val <= alpha:
            rejections += 1
    return rejections / n_simulations


np.random.seed(42)
fpr_two_sided = simulate_AA(p_shared=0.10, n_per_group=2000,
                            n_simulations=2000, alternative="two-sided")
fpr_one_sided = simulate_AA(p_shared=0.10, n_per_group=2000,
                            n_simulations=2000, alternative="less")

print(f"Two-sided FPR: {fpr_two_sided:.3f}  (target ≈ 0.05)")
print(f"One-sided FPR: {fpr_one_sided:.3f}  (target ≈ 0.05)")
```

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## What to do if FPR is far from $\alpha$
- **FPR clearly above** $\alpha$ → the infrastructure invents effects.
  Likely causes: peeking, broken randomization, data from the two arms
  coming from different sources.
- **FPR clearly below** $\alpha$ → overly conservative test. Possibly a
  wrong SE formula or continuity correction applied where it shouldn't
  be.
- Running A/B on such infrastructure is wasted traffic.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Your turn 7
Run an A/A on a SMALL sample ($n=50$ per group), 2000 simulations. Does
FPR still hover near $0.05$? Does the normal approximation hold when
$n \cdot p = 5$?
:::

---

# The full experiment cycle

Walk through the experiment "the way it happens at work": planning
first, then a simulated launch, then a report.

## Step 1. Planning

```{python}
#| label: run-plan

mu0           = 0.10
business_mde  = 0.01           # +1 pp — the smallest interesting effect
alpha         = 0.05
target_power  = 0.80

N = plan_ab_experiment(p1=mu0, mde=business_mde,
                       alpha=alpha, target_power=target_power,
                       alternative="less")

print(f"Plan: run the test on N = {N} users PER GROUP")
print(f"      (min detectable effect = +{business_mde*100:.0f} pp, power = {target_power})")
```

## Step 2. Simulated launch

We simulate a reality where the new button **truly** lifts conversion to
11.5% (unknown to the analyst in advance).

```{python}
#| label: run-sim

np.random.seed(2026)

true_p_control = 0.10
true_p_test    = 0.115

sample_control = binom.rvs(1, true_p_control, size=N)
sample_test    = binom.rvs(1, true_p_test,    size=N)

x1 = int(sample_control.sum())
x2 = int(sample_test.sum())

print(f"Control: {x1} repeat orders out of {N} (p_hat = {x1/N:.4f})")
print(f"Test:    {x2} repeat orders out of {N} (p_hat = {x2/N:.4f})")
```

## Step 3. Analysis

```{python}
#| label: run-test

result = my_ab_test(x1, N, x2, N,
                    alternative="less",
                    alpha=alpha,
                    target_power=target_power)

print(result["summary"])
```

## Step 4. Stakeholder report

::: {.border}
**Summary of the "1-click reorder" A/B test**

- Observed repeat-order conversion:
  control = **`{python} round(result['p1']*100, 2)`%**,
  test = **`{python} round(result['p2']*100, 2)`%**.
- Lift: **`{python} f"{result['lift']*100:+.2f}"` pp**.
- 95% CI for lift:
  **[`{python} f"{-result['ci_diff'][1]*100:+.2f}"`, `{python} f"{-result['ci_diff'][0]*100:+.2f}"`] pp**.
- Test at $\alpha=0.05$:
  **`{python} 'reject H0 - evidence of a lift' if result['reject'] else 'do not reject H0 - not enough evidence'`**
  ($p = $ `{python} round(result['p_value'], 4)`).
- Plan power at the observed data:
  `{python} round(result['power_at_observed'], 3)`.

**Recommendation:** if the lower CI bound for the lift exceeds the
business MDE (+1 pp) --- ship it. Otherwise --- collect more data or go
back to the design stage.
:::

---

# Pitfalls of interpretation

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Three sentences a manager should never hear

❌ "$p = 0.03$, so with probability 97% our button is better."
   Conflates $p$-value with $P(H_1 \mid \text{data})$.

❌ "The test showed no significant effect, so the button does not work."
   Absence of evidence ≠ evidence of absence. Always ask about **MDE**:
   did the test even have a chance to detect the effect?

❌ "Let's stop --- we already see $p < 0.05$ at 500 users."
   Classic *peeking*: it inflates the FPR.
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## What the CI does **not** mean

❌ "There is a 95% probability that the true $\mu$ lies in this interval."

✅ "If we repeated the experiment many times, 95% of the constructed
   intervals would cover the true $\mu$."

The interval is random; the parameter is not.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Pooled vs unpooled SE --- do not mix them up
- **Test of $H_0: p_1 = p_2$:** pooled SE (assume equal proportions).
- **CI for $p_1 - p_2$:** unpooled SE (describe the observed difference,
  no assumption).

A common bug in home-grown A/B calculators.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Multiple testing
If you simultaneously test $k$ variants at level $\alpha=0.05$, the
probability of at least one false positive grows as $1 - (1-\alpha)^k$.
For $k=3$ that's already $\approx 14\%$. Simple fixes:

- **Bonferroni:** use $\alpha/k$ for each test (conservative).
- **Holm--Bonferroni** or **Benjamini--Hochberg** (FDR control) ---
  less conservative. Implementations:
  `statsmodels.stats.multitest.multipletests`.
:::

---

# Pre-launch self-check {.unnumbered}

- [ ] Is it clear **which business decision** the test result will drive?
- [ ] Is the baseline $\mu_0$ known, and **where** does it come from?
- [ ] One-sided or two-sided test --- and **why**?
- [ ] What is the **business MDE**?
- [ ] Is $N$ calculated for that MDE with power $\geq 0.8$?
- [ ] How will we report results: $p$-value, CI, or both?
- [ ] **A/A passed** on this infrastructure (FPR $\approx \alpha$)?
- [ ] Pooled SE for the test, unpooled SE for the CI --- not mixed up?
- [ ] Is $\alpha$ adjusted for multiple testing if you test several variants?
- [ ] What do we do if the test is **inconclusive** (no rejection but
  also not powered enough)?

If any of these is "no" --- it is **too early** to launch.
